title: Fix variable randomization to better differ by seed (#6945)
body:
Fix #6945.


This fixes the issue of bad randomness in `VL_SCOPED_RAND_RESET_I` when changing the seed through `+verilator+seed+<seed>`. This is done by changing the PRNG algorithm from Xoroshiro128+ to Xoroshiro128**, which fixes the issue where the lower bits of the generated values have low linear complexity, and by seeding the initial state using `splitmix64` to avoid propagating the linear structure of the original seed.

I added a test that checks whether multiple runs generate enough unique combinations. If the randomization is close to ideal, it should fail at a rate of about 1 in 12 trillion runs. The commit before the fix, and also the version where I only change the algorithm to Xoroshiro128**, fails consistently (I didn’t measure the rate). Seeding `VlRNG` with `splitmix64` is enough to fix the test, I don’t know how to test for the low linear complexity of the lowest bit when using Xoroshiro128+.

I could not find a way to capture the stdout of the test execution, so the test writes what it needs to a file.

---

I am not a specialist in random number generators, but from what I’ve researched, the problem we are seeing in #6945 is roughly as follows:

The Xoroshiro series of pseudo-random number generators updates its internal state through a series of linear operations over GF(2) (the Galois Field with 2 elements, basically bitwise arithmetic where linear operations are XOR and bit permutations).

When we seed the internal state with the expression `salt ^ seed` for different seed values, we are basically applying a linear operation (XOR, in this case) to the initial state. Since the transition to each next PRNG state is also a linear operation, applying a linear operation to the initial state has the same effect as applying a corresponding linear transform to every subsequent state, and potentially to the generated values as well.

Xoroshiro128+ generates values using a non-linear operation before returning them: the PLUS operator (`m_state[0] + m_state[1]`). However, this operation is still linear for bit 0, so if we sample the lowest bit from the generated values (which we do for 1-bit signals), we are effectively sampling a linear function of the initial seed.

To fix that, we replace the output function from `+` with a multiplication, followed by a rotation, followed by another multiplication (which is where the name `Xoroshiro128**` comes from). From my tests, this output function still does not have enough linear complexity to fully eliminate the problem caused by seeding the initial state with a linear operation, so we use a stronger non-linear operation, `splitmix64`, to fully eliminate that problem (as the reference implementation suggests).

I dug up the above theory after testing a couple of PRNGs and hash functions to see which ones worked, and I was surprised by how poorly some of them performed (including the rehashing approach that @wsnyder suggested on #6945) compared to others. Here is the code I used (it is in Rust): https://gist.github.com/Rodrigodd/32295033b7ba7d95529a5e928d81234e

Note that just seeding the initial state with `splitmix64` is enough to fix #6945. Replacing Xoroshiro128+ with Xoroshiro128** was done mainly because I kept finding mentions of the low linear complexity of its lower bits, which I initially thought to be the cause of the problem.
